coredata.dc:title: A sensitive biosensor based on a ferrocene-marked adapter for the fluorescence detection of platelet-derived growth factor BB
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xia
Wen, Xiaoye
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent nanometer biosensor integrating a β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon dot (β-CD-CD) fluorescent nanoprobe and ferrocene-marked adapter (ssDNA-Fc) was developed and used to measure platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)-BB. When no PDGF-BB was added, ssDNA-Fc induced the enhanced fluorescence of β-CD-CD nanoprobes. After adding PDGF-BB, ssDNA-Fc specifically bonded with PDGF-BB. Meanwhile, the β-CD-CDs and Fc in ssDNA-Fc initiated host–guest identification, which then induced photoinduced electron transfer and decreased fluorescence, thereby contributing to the high-sensitivity monitoring of PDGF-BB. This novel approach showed a wide linear range of 10 pg mL−1 to 8 μg mL−1 and a low detection limit of 6 pg mL−1 (RSD = ±4.6%). Moreover, the spiked recovery rate in human serum was 98.2%–106.2% (RSD ≤ ±3.79%), indicating that this method can be used to monitor PDGF-BB in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Adapter
Host-guest identification
Platelet-derived growth factor BB
β-Cyclodextrin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity, intervention, and stability of fluorescent probes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR and (B) UV–vis spectra of N-CDs, β-CD, and β-CD-CDs. (C) TEM image and (D) Size distribution histogram of β-CD-CDs.
Sensing mechanism of the proposed β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobe for PDGF-BB detection.

(A) Influence of ssDNA-Fc concentration of β-CD-CD (20 μg mL−1); (B) effect of β-CD-CD (20 μg mL−1) and PDGF-BB (1 ng mL−1) addition on the fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence spectra of β-CD-CDs (curve a), β-CD-CDs + ssDNA-Fc (curve b), ssDNA-Fc + PDGF-BB + β-CD-CDs (curve c), and ssDNA-Fc + β-CD-CDs + PDGF-BB (curve d). ssDNA-Fc (0.4 μM); (C) temperature of ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB specific binding; (D) time of fluorescence enhancement phase (Time 1) and time of ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB specific binding (Time 2).

Concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity of β-CD-CDs (20 μg mL−1) in the presence of PDGF-BB (0, 10 pg mL−1, 100 pg mL−1, 1 ng mL−1, 10 ng mL−1, 100 ng mL−1, 1 μg mL−1,5 μg mL−1, and 8 μg mL−1) and calibration curve. All solutions were prepared in PBS at pH 7.4.
(A) Effects of PDGF-BB and other proteins on fluorescence intensity. (B) Effects of mixture containing PDGF-BB and foreign substances on the fluorescent probe system.
Recovery (Mean ± s:n = 3) for PDGF-BB measurement in human serum.
Comparison of fluorescent probe system with other methods for the PDGF-BB determination.

row.entry: Based on β-cyclodextrine/graphitic carbon nitride composite
Dual signal amplification strategy using hydroxyapatite NPs
A background current-eliminated aptameric sensing platform
Exo ΙΙΙ- aided signal amplification strategy
Based on β-cyclodextrin-modified N-doped carbon dot fluorescent probes

head.ce:title: A sensitive biosensor based on a ferrocene-marked adapter for the fluorescence detection of platelet-derived growth factor BB

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent nanometer biosensor integrating a β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon dot (β-CD-CD) fluorescent nanoprobe and ferrocene-marked adapter (ssDNA-Fc) was developed and used to measure platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)-BB. When no PDGF-BB was added, ssDNA-Fc induced the enhanced fluorescence of β-CD-CD nanoprobes. After adding PDGF-BB, ssDNA-Fc specifically bonded with PDGF-BB. Meanwhile, the β-CD-CDs and Fc in ssDNA-Fc initiated host–guest identification, which then induced photoinduced electron transfer and decreased fluorescence, thereby contributing to the high-sensitivity monitoring of PDGF-BB. This novel approach showed a wide linear range of 10 pg mL−1 to 8 μg mL−1 and a low detection limit of 6 pg mL−1 (RSD = ±4.6%). Moreover, the spiked recovery rate in human serum was 98.2%–106.2% (RSD ≤ ±3.79%), indicating that this method can be used to monitor PDGF-BB in human serum.

Scheme 1. Schematic of β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobe for PDGF-BB detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)-BB is a well-known serum cytokine that regulates cell growth, division, and transformation [1,2]. PDGF-BB is a critical cancer-related protein marker in the human body with direct and indirect participation in cell proliferation, cell transformation, and tumor growth[3,4]. PDGF-BB is also a good predictor and indicator of early renal function deterioration, including diabetes, prostate cancer, and breast cancer[5,6]. Precise qualitative and quantitative detection of PDGF-BB can help in the early diagnosis and treatment of relevant diseases [7], as well as contribute to bioresearch and clinical diagnosis. Currently, various approaches such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [8], antibody-based radioisotopes [9], molecular fluorescence technique [10], high-sensitivity electrochemical process [11], colorimetry [12], Raman method [13], and chemiluminescence immunoassay [14] are used to measure PDGF-BB. Despite the high sensitivity of these methods, they exhibit several limitations, such as high cost, instrumental complexity, and high requirement for operators. Thus, designing and creating convenient, sensitive, and selective biosensors with fast response and low cost are of great importance.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) [15] are novel nanomaterials attracting extensive interest because of their advantages, such as high water solubility, low toxicity, high biological compatibility, light stability, abundant electron–hole exciton pairs, and functionality [16,17]. These CDs are favorable electron donors or receptors with strong ability of photoinduced electron transfer (PET)[18], synthesis and marked ability [19], and optical properties [20]. Moreover, CDs have wide-ranging applications, such as biological imaging [21], food safety [22], biomedicine[23], and chemical sensing. However, CDs from direct preparation usually have insufficient fluorescent quantum yield (FLQY < 50%) and sensitivity. For this reason, we doped N atoms containing quinquevalent electrons into the CDs[24,25], forming N-doped CDs to improve the FLQY[26]. β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) is a ring-shaped oligonucleotide characterized by unique hydrophobic cavity, hydrophilic external structure, high water solubility [27], biocompatibility, and low toxicity [28]. The hydrophobic cavity of β-CD enables organic substances, inorganic materials, and biological molecules with appropriate sizes to be selectively coated through physical and chemical forces [29], thereby forming stable host–guest inclusion complexes, such as ferrocene[30] and adapters [31]. Accordingly, β-CD is attracting considerable attention, but its chemical inertia complicates the qualitative or quantitative analysis of substances that enter the cavity of β-CD. Nevertheless, β-CD functionalized CDs that integrate the properties of supramolecular compounds and nanometer properties of CDs can be prepared [30,32]. Molecular recognition and qualitative or quantitative detection can be achieved through PET or fluorescence resonance energy transfer of CDs.

Adapters are a type of functional nucleic acid macromolecules with specificity and affinity equivalent to antigen antibodies and can bind with various target substances, such as organic micromolecules [33], metal ions [34], proteins [35], and living cells [36]. Adapters with low cost, high stability, and high target-identification ability can be easily synthesized, marked, and modified in recent years; thus, they are extensively used in biomedicine, such as in biosensor development and nanoparticle-based drug delivery[37]. Ferrocene (Fc) is a common substance [38] that can penetrate the holes of β-CD-CDs through hydrophobic interaction, forming a stable 1:1 complex compound [39] and thus shortening the distance from the CDs of the probes [30]. When the distance between Fc and CDs is sufficiently close, under fluorescent irradiation, the excited electrons of CDs migrate to the empty orbit of Fc and do not return to the ground state, thereby inducing the PET and weakening the fluorescence of CDs [30,40]. When Fc is marked onto an adapter (e.g., single-stranded DNA (ssDNA)), the double properties of these substances can be obtained, including the specific molecular-recognition ability of the adapter and the electron signal transduction ability of Fc [41,42]. Both substances can interact with the holes of β-CD through host–guest identification, forming inclusions [31,39,43].

ssDNA and Fc can compete for host–guest in the cavity of β-CD, and ssDNA can block the PET between Fc and CDs. On the basis of this phenomenon and the double properties of ssDNA-Fc, we designed a novel fluorescent nano-bioaptamer sensor for the highly sensitive and rapid detection of PDGF-BB.

PDGF-BB was purchased from Cyagen Biosciences, Suzhou, China. PDGF-AA and PDGF-AB were purchased from Beijing Biolab Technology Co., Ltd. Mono(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-CD was purchased from Shandong Binzhou Zhiyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine, phosphate buffer solution (PBS; pH 7.4; containing 137 mM NaCl; 2.68 mM KCl, 8.1 mM Na2HPO4, and 1.76 mM KH2PO4), (ferrocenylmethyl)trimethylammonium iodide (Fc), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (China). The other materials included MgCl2, KCl, urea, NaNO3, and NaCl (Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Company, China); ascorbic acid (AA), dl-tryptophan (dl-Trp), and dl-tyrosine (dl-Tyr) (China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation, Shanghai); aysozymes (egg white, LZM), myoglobin (Mb), and bovine immunoglobulin (lgG) (Shanghai Yuanye BioTech Co., Ltd., China); 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethyl amine)propyl (EDC HCl) and n-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (GL Biochem Ltd., Shanghai, China); and serum (Chongqing Manuik Technology Co., Ltd., China). All these agents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Dialysis bags (cutoff molecular weight = 3.5 kDa) were offered by Union Car-bide Co. (USA). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was generated with a Milli-Q system (USA). All oligonucleotides were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) and dissolved in PBS (pH 7.4) before use. The sequences were listed below:
ssDNA: 5′-CAGGCTACGGCACGTAGAGCATCACCATGATCCTG-3′;
ssDNA-Fc: 5′-CAGGCTACGGCACGTAGAGCATCACCATGATCCTG-Fc-3′;

The fluorescence was measured with an LS-55 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) in 1.0 × 1.0 cm2 quartz cuvettes. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectra were detected on a Cary 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The sample solutions were dripped onto copper meshes covered by amorphous porous carbon films. Samples were analyzed with a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI, USA; 200 kV accelerated voltage) and a Niocolet 380 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer. The chemical compositions were characterized with a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, USA).

N-CDs with high FLQY, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and high hydrophilicity were prepared from one-step hydrothermal synthesis in a Teflon-to-stainless steel autoclave. Approximately 1.0 mL of ethylenediamine and 2.0 g of anhydrous CA were added to 40 mL of ultrapure water with stirring until complete dissolution based on a previously reported method [44] with slight modifications, followed by hydrothermal processing at 150 °C in a 100 mL polytetrafluorethylene-lined autoclave for 2 h. After natural cooling, the resulting dark brown solution was dialyzed and purified in ultrapure water for 48 h at room temperature (RT) (3.5 kDa) to remove impurities. Brown solids were obtained after filtering through 0.22 μm films and vacuum freeze drying.

β-CD-CDs were manufactured from the condensation between the –COOH of N-CDs and the –NH2 of β-CD on the basis of a reported method [45]. NHS (51 mg) and EDC HCl (43 mg) were placed in 30 mL of a 0.5 mg mL−1 CD solution, mixed with 200 mg of β-CD after 1 h of full agitation, and stirred at RT for 12 h to ensure sufficient reaction. After the reaction, the mixed solution was purified for 48 h in dialysis bags (3.5 kDa). Finally, β-CD-CDs were acquired after freeze drying.

Approximately 0.4 μM ssDNA-Fc and a gradient of PDGF-BB solutions (0, 10, and 100 pg mL−1; 1, 10, and 100 ng mL−1; 1, 5, and 8 μg mL−1) were successively placed in 10 mL colorimetric tubes. After 50 min of incubation at 37 °C, 20 μg mL−1 of β-CD-CDs was added. All tubes were diluted by PBS (pH 7.4) to 5 mL before reaction at RT for 80 min. The fluorescence at the emission of 445 nm was recorded with the fluorescence spectrometer (excitation and emission slit widths at 5 and 10 nm, respectively) under the optimal excited condition of 350 nm and scanning range of 350–650 nm. The experimental data were expressed as relative fluorescence ΔF/F0 to avoid background interference, where ΔF is the difference value of (F–F0) (F and F0 are the fluorescence with and without PDGF-BB, respectively).

After centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatants of the serum samples were gathered and diluted 100 fold. Approximately 20 μg mL−1 β-CD-CDs and 50 μL of human serum were successively added to 10 mL colorimetric tubes with uniform shaking. After 50 min of incubation, ssDNA-Fc (0.4 μM) and PDGF-BB (50 pg mL−1; 0.5 and 50 ng mL−1) were added. All tubes were diluted to 5 mL and cultured at ambient temperature for 80 min, followed by fluorescence-intensity recording. Each experiment was repeated thrice.
The β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobes were characterized by FTIR, UV–vis spectrum, TEM, and XPS.

The FTIR spectrum of β-CD-CDs exhibited three intense peaks at 1156, 1083, and 1031 cm −1 (Fig. 1 A), which were characteristic of β-CD and caused by the coupling extensional vibration of C–O–H, C–C, and C–O and the asymmetric glucosidic bond vibration of C–O–C. The peak at 1717 cm −1 almost disappeared in CDs due to the stretching vibration of –COOH. However, two new peaks at 1684 and 1554 cm −1 appearing in β-CD-CDs were induced by the stretching vibration of CO in –CO–NH–. The peak at 3109 cm −1 disappeared in CDs, and those of β-CD-CDs at 3257 and 2927 cm −1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of N–H and C–H, respectively. These results suggested that CDs and β-CD were effectively coupled and condensed into the –CO–NH– covalent bond, generating β-CD-CD nanocomposites. However, the physical adsorption of β-CD was not observed.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, β-CD, and β-CD-CDs are shown in Fig. 1B. As is well-known, the absorption peak at 245 nm of CDs is ascribed to the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores, while the strong peak at 345 nm is caused by the n-π* transition of CDs [46]. Since β-CD is functionalized via the amido bond (–CO–NH-) onto N-CDs, the optical properties of N-CDs are not changed [45]. Hence, we think the strong peaks of the β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobe at 275 and 340 nm can be attributed to CDs. The β-CD-CDs in the water solutions showed light blue fluorescence at approximately 445 nm, which blue-shifted by 20 nm compared with CDs (475 nm). This blue shift that occurred after the introduction of CDs was ascribed to the high electron affinity of N atoms [26] to form amide groups. The fluorescent quantum yield Φ of β-CD-CDs under excitation at 350 nm was calculated with rhodamine B as the control (Table S1). Results showed the fluorescent quantum yield of β-CD-CDs (62.3%) was slightly larger than that of N-CDs (58.4% [44]). One appropriate explanation was that the modification with β-CD may passivate the surface of CD, leading to increasing quantum yield [47,48].

The sizes and morphology of the β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobes were observed via TEM (Fig. 1C). The TEM images of β-CD-CDs and CDs were similar to those of the β-CD-CDs’ spherical grains with monodispersity and uniform sizes. The nanoparticle size-distribution histogram (Fig. 1D), which was determined by randomly counting 100 particles, exhibited that the size rangesof the β-CD-CDs were 7–10 nm. This size was slightly larger than those of CDs because of the relatively large sizes of β-CD (approximately 1.5 nm in diameter) [49]. The process of loading onto the surface of CDs enlarged the original sizes. The high-resolution TEM images showed that the lattice fringes of β-CD-CDs were evident and consistent with those of CDs, indicating that the newly formed β-CD-CDs retained the nanostructure of CDs.

The chemical compositions of β-CD-CDs were characterized by XPS (Fig. S1). The β-CD-CDs consisted mostly of C (49.63%), N (4.59%), and O (45.78%), thereby confirming that the N and O functional groups were enriched on the carbon substrate.

The N-CDs were connected onto β-CD through a –CO–NH– covalent bond resulting from the reaction between –COOH and –NH2, forming β-CD-CD fluorescent bioprobes. After the functionalization of CDs by β-CD, the high water solubility of CDs and the hydrophilic outer surface of β-CD improved the water solubility of these supramolecular structures. However, the hydrophobic environment in the cavity of β-CD was also attracted to the surface of N-CDs [30]. Specifically, the high molecular selectivity in the cavity of β-CD was introduced into the system. This hydrophobic cavity can be used by various organic substances, inorganic substances, and biomolecules (e.g., adapters [31]) to form stable host–guest inclusion composites.

As shown in Fig. 2 , when ssDNA-Fc and β-CD-CD were blended, the ssDNA and Fc at the two ends both can enter the cavity of cyclodextrin. In other words, β-CD can include both ssDNA and Fc. Considering that the entry of Fc into the cavity of cyclodextrin can lead to photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the electron transfer between Fc and CDs can also reportedly lead to the loss of hydrophobicity of the β-CD cavity and weakened complexation ability of the charged Fc+ [50–52]. ssDNA, after being added to the system, competed with Fc for the cavity of β-CD and finally replaced Fc+ to enter the β-CD cavity. Accordingly, after a certain period of incubation, ssDNA substituted the Fc that had already entered the cavity of β-CD-CDs, forming a new β-CD-CD-ssDNA inclusion compound and enhancing the fluorescence. However, after the specific binding between ssDNA and PDGF-BB, steric hindrance prevented the successful entry of one end of ssDNA into the β-CD-CD cavity. Consequently, the exposed end of Fc successfully entered this cavity and avoided substitution, which induced PET and led to fluorescence quenching. Throughout this process, ssDNA-Fc served as the specific recognizer of PDGF-BB and electron acceptor of β-CD-CD fluorescent nanomaterials (Fig. S2).

We have previously observed that the β-CD cavity can compete with Fc for host–guest identification, and PET is induced after Fc approaches the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD [30]. In other words, the surface-photoinduced excitation electrons of CDs rapidly migrate to the empty orbit of Fc+ and do not return, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the nanoprobes. As reported, β-CD can bind firmly, in an uncharged state, with Fc and its derivatives, but after electron transfer, due to the loss of hydrophobicity in the cavity of β-CD, the binding stability between the charged Fc+ and the β-CD was largely weakened, and the Fc+ and β-CD may dissociate [53,54]. Moreover, Fc+ can be removed from cavities after the host-guest recognition of β-CD with other molecules. In addition to the bases of ssDNA that were chelated by the cavities of β-CD, the phosphoric acid part of ssDNA can also bind with β-CD via hydrogen bonding [55,56]. After this process, the bases cannot be easily separated from the cavities of β-CD, and the stability of β-CD-ssDNA clathrates was further enhanced. Research has shown that the stability of the clathrate compounds was decided by the escape rate of guest molecules from cavities [56]. Hence, we think the binding between the β-CD-CD nanoprobe and ssDNA was more stable than the binding between β-CD-CD and the charged Fc+. To validate this thought, we added ssDNA and (ferrocenylmethyl)trimethylammonium iodide (Fc) separately into the β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobe and observed the reactions. When only Fc was added, the fluorescence was decreased, and when only ssDNA was added, the fluorescence was enhanced (Fig. S3A). When Fc was added first, the fluorescence intensity was weakened, and after ssDNA was added later, the fluorescence rose instead. We think this is because ssDNA and Fc competed for the cavities of β-CD, and when ssDNA entered the cavity of β-CD, the Fc+ was removed from the cavities; moreover, the PET was cut off and thereby the fluorescence of the β-CD-CDs fluorescence probe was restored. To eliminate the interference from CDs, we designed another experiment (Fig. S3B). When only CDs and Fc existed in the system, Fc+ only slightly quenched the fluorescence of CDs, and after ssDNA was added, the quenched fluorescence of CDs basically cannot be restored. It was indicated though CDs can induce photo-induced electron-transfer with Fc+ and thereby reduced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, if the hydrophobic cavities supplied by β-CD for host-guest competition between ssDNA and Fc+ were unavailable, the ssDNA cannot block the PET between CDs and Fc+, so the fluorescence intensity cannot be recovered. When only ssDNA was added to the CDs fluorescence system, the fluorescence was not significantly enhanced. This result implies that the functionalization of CD by β-CD not only offered ssDNA with an anchor point in the main molecular cavity, but also acted as a bridge of electronic signal transduction between CD and ssDNA.

As reported, when bases, under the stack state of single-strand structure (ssDNA), entered the cavity of β-CD, color enhancement will occur [55,57]. When ssDNA was added to the β-CD-CD nanoprobe, fluorescence enhancement also occurred (Fig. S3A), which is consistent with the phenomenon in Fig. 3 A that after the addition of ssDNA-Fc, the fluorescence intensity of the system was gradually enhanced with the rise of ssDNA-Fc concentration. The reason was that the cavity of β-CD possessed excellent light sensitiveness and unique host-guest recognizing ability [31,58]. We all know the cavity of β-CD is like a unique microenvironment of organic solvents and when the molecules of the guest were clathrated, the cavity can prevent the external quenchers from quenching the fluorescence of the guest. Moreover, the movement freedom of guest molecules in the cavity was reduced, the rotation was blocked and thereby polarity in the local cavity environment was significantly deceased, which also led to spectral drift, variation of excited-state lifetime, and other light sensitive phenomena in the system and thereby changed the fluorescence intensity in the system [59,60]. In addition, when CDs were modified by β-CD onto the β-CD-CDs fluorescent nanoprobe, due to the surface passivation of CDs, not only the fluorescent quantum yield of the probe was raised [61], but also the effect of external quenchers on the electron center of CDs was weakened. However, after the negatively-charged ssDNA was captured by the cavity of β-CD, the surface electron transfer of CDs was further inhibited. The above reasons all can enhance the fluorescence intensity in the system to some extent.

On the basis of the phenomenon of ssDNA and Fc competing for host–guest in the cavity of β-CD, as well as the double properties of ssDNA-Fc (including the specific molecular-recognition ability of ssDNA and the electron signal transduction ability of Fc), we believed that one end of ssDNA can completely enter the cavity of β-CD in the absence of PDGF-BB, which enhanced the fluorescence of β-CD-CD nanoprobes. In the presence of PDGF-BB, the ssDNA of ssDNA-Fc can specifically bond with PDGF-BB. Consequently, β-CD-CDs can include one end of Fc on ssDNA-Fc, inducing PET to weaken the fluorescence intensity and thus accounting for the high-sensitivity detection of PDGF-BB.

We selected the optimal PBS for PDGF-BB (pH 7.4) and optimized the dosage of ssDNA-Fc, the order of addition of β-CD-CDs and PDGF-BB, culture temperature, and reaction time to enhance PDGF-BB detection.

We evaluated the effects of the ssDNA-Fc concentration on the fluorescence of β-CD-CDs (Fig. 3A). The fluorescence at 445 nm of solutions mixed from 20 μg mL −1 β-CD-CDs and ssDNA-Fc (0–0.50 μM) was detected by the fluorescence spectrometer at the optimal excitation wavelength (λEx). With the appropriate dosage of ssDNA-Fc, the mixed solution was diluted to 5 mL by using the PBS (pH 7.4) fully blended and cultured at ambient temperature for 80 min. With increased aptamer concentration from 0.05 μM to 0.4 μM, ΔF/F0 increased and reached the maximum at 0.4 μM. However, with further increased aptamer concentration from 0.4 μM to 0.5 μM (inset of Fig. 3A), the ΔF/F0 decreased. Such decrease may be attributed to the space steric effect [58] and to the interentangling of excessive single-strand ssDNA-Fc [62]. The interentangled ssDNA-Fc prevented the purine bases from sufficient exposure and thereby decreased the probability of entrance to the cavities of β-CD.

The addition order of β-CD-CDs and PDGF-BB directly affected the detection results (Fig. 3B). When only β-CD-CDs existed, the fluorescence intensity was moderate (curve a). After adding ssDNA-Fc, fluorescence intensity was enhanced (curve b). When β-CD-CDs were added after incubation in a water bath at 37 °C for 50 min with the coexistence of ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB and then cultivated at RT for 80 min, the fluorescence intensity was weakened (curve c). However, the fluorescence was intensified (curve d) and was close to the case when no PDGF-BB was added (curve b) when the addition order of PDGF-BB and β-CD-CDs was altered. This change was attributed to the ssDNA-Fc entering the cavity of β-CD-CDs, which may hinder the binding between ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB to a certain extent [63]. For this reason, β-CD-CDs should be added after the specific binding between PDGF-BB and ssDNA-Fc during PDGF-BB detection.

Fig. 3C shows the influence of temperatures on the reaction rate and effect of specific binding between ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB. The specific binding rate and effect were optimized at 37 °C. The effect of reaction time was studied from two aspects (Fig. 3D). During the stage wherein ssDNA-Fc enhanced the fluorescence of β-CD-CDs, a certain amount of ssDNA-Fc was used to prolong the incubation time. First, we measured the fluorescence of solutions (5.0 mL, pH 7.4) mixed from 20 μg mL −1 β-CD-CDs and 0.4 μM of ssDNA–Fc. Fc and the adapter competed for host–guest in the β-CD cavity. After 80 min of incubation, regardless of which substance first entered the cavity, one end of the adapter replaced the Fc of the other end after entry into the β-CD–CD cavity (Time 1). During the stage involving the ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB specific binding phase, we recorded the fluorescent signals after 80 min of reaction at RT after ssDNA-Fc (0.4 μM) and PDGF-BB (1 ng mL −1 ) were added into colorimetric tubes and when β-CD-CDs (20 μg mL −1 ) were added after 0–100 min of incubation in a water bath at 37 °C. The ΔF/F0 curve showed a turning point after 50 min of incubation with ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB, and ΔF/F0 stabilized with time (Time 2). Thus, we collected fluorescent signals under the following conditions: β-CD-CDs were added after ssDNA-Fc and PDGF-BB were cultured at 37 °C for 50 min, with 80 min of reaction at RT.

Under the optimal experiment conditions, a β-CD-CD fluorescence nanoprobe for PDGF-BB detection was created, and the standard curve was plotted according to the association between the PDGF-BB content and the fluorescence intensity (ΔF/F0). The fluorescence intensity was gradually weakened with the increased PDGF-BB content (Fig. 4 ). A logarithm curve between the ΔF/F0 variation and the PDGF-BB content was plotted, which showed an evident linearity within a certain range (10 pg mL −1 –8 μg mL −1 , Fig. 4): the linear equation was ΔF/F0 = -0.034lgCPDGF-BB -0.01 (R2 = 0.995); the limit of detection was (3 s/k) 6 pg mL −1 (RSD = ±4.6%), where s was the relative standard deviation of 11 blank signals with the absence of PDGF-BB, and k was the slope of the standard curve at the concentration of 10 pg mL −1 to 8 μg mL −1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, intervention, and stability of fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: We incubated ssDNA-Fc for 50 min with 1 ng mL−1 PDGF-BB, PDGF-AA, and PDGF-AB, as well as 10-fold concentration of Mb, BSA, lgG, LZM, or their mixtures, to test the selectivity and specificity of the new system. β-CD-CD (20 μg mL−1) was then added under incubation for another 80 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was recorded. After the reactions, the PDGF-BB showed high specificity because of the strong specific binding between the PDGF-BB and the adapter (Fig. 5 A), consistent with other similar methods [11,64]. This result suggested that the new fluorescence sensor has high selective specificity.

Certain metal ions and common biomolecules in biological fluids were selected to study their interference on the PDGF-BB detection by the new β-CD-CD fluorescence nanoprobes (variation of fluorescence intensity ≤ ±5% was considered as no interference) to determine the practicability of our new method. In the presence of 1 ng mL−1 PDGF-BB, the addition of 100-fold concentration of Na+, Mg2+, urea, glucose (Glu), or fructose (Fru), 50-fold concentration of K+, NO3 −, glycine, Ca2+, CA, or CO3 2−, or 10-fold concentration of dl-Trp or AA, or fivefold concentration of glutathione, or dl-Tyr insignificantly intervened the fluorescent signals of PDGF-BB (Fig. 5B). This result suggested that the new method can be used to effectively measure PDGF-BB in complex samples.

With the new 20 μg mL−1 β-CD-CD fluorescence nanoprobe, the fluorescence at 445 nm was detected under the optimal excitation peak. The resulting probe solutions were placed in an icebox at 4 °C and detected every other day under the same conditions. Results showed that after 5 weeks, the fluorescence intensity declined by only 4.19% (RSD ≤ ±2.88%) (Fig. S4), indicating the high stability of the probe. Detection was conducted in triplicate.

We designed a human serum spiked recovery experiment that covered the physiological contents in cancer patients (0.02–0.4 nM) to accurately investigate the practical application of the β-CD-CD fluorescence nanoprobes in PDGF-BB detection [11]. Approximately 50 pg mL−1; 0.5 and 50 ng mL−1 PDGF-BB was added to the human serum samples after dilution by 100-fold, and then fluorescence intensity was detected (Table 1 ). After three repeated tests, the average spiked recovery rates were 98.2%–106.2% (RSD ≤ ±3.79%), indicating that the β-CD-CD fluorescence nanoprobes can be used to measure PDGF-BB in real samples.

The new method has a significantly lower detection limit than colorimetry [12], Raman test [13], voltammetry [64], luminescence method [65], chemiluminescence method [14], BLI [66], and other fluorescence methods [67] but a higher one than electroluminescence [11], the Abe method [32], field-effect transistor method [68], and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [69] (Table 2 ). Nevertheless, the new method had a significantly wide linear range and covered the range of physiological levels in cancer patients (0.02–0.4 nM) [11]. This result suggested that the new biosensors showed a low detection limit and a broad range of linearity and can detect PDGF-BB in real cases.

Novel β-CD-CDs were synthesized starting from β-CD-functionalized environment-friendly N-doped CDs as fluorescent nanomaterials and used to measure PDGF-BB on the basis of the specific binding between the adapter and PDGF-BB and the photoelectron migration between cyclodextrin cavity and ferrocene. The PDGF-BB detection range widened according to the adapter-induced enhancement of fluorescence in β-CD-CDs, showing a linear range of 10 pg mL−1–8 μg mL−1 and a low detection limit of 6 pg mL−1 (RSD = ±4.6%). The new method showed high stability, selectivity, and anti-interference ability. However, its detection limit was not the lowest among all similar methods and still had a broad linear range. The new method was convenient and environment friendly, effective for detecting real samples, and did not require any complex preprocessing. The new method also properly integrated the macrocyclic compound β-CD, biomacromolecules (PDGF-BB and adapter), and PET and promoted the development of potential biomacromolecule-detection methods. Moreover, the application field of supramolecular-compound-functionalized nanomaterials was expanded for biological detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Graduate Scientific Research Foundation of Shanxi Normal University (0109–01053002).
